g4_revenue_trend.description: Revenue rose from $66.8B in FY2018 to $67.7B in FY2019 to $71.0B in FY2020. The portfolio was plainly not in structural decline.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: Gross margin improved from roughly 48.5% in FY2018 to 48.6% in FY2019 to 50.3% in FY2020. No deterioration was visible.

g7_moat.description: P&G still had one of the strongest portfolios of household brands in the world, backed by advertising scale, shelf presence, and global distribution. The only real issue was paying a premium consumer-staples multiple for that quality.

quality_multiple_note.summary: P&G's low earnings yield was a pure quality multiple on a globally dominant branded-consumer platform.
quality_multiple_note.reason_for_low_yield: This was not a dubious story stock. It was a great business already priced like one in a low-rate environment.
quality_multiple_note.decision_impact: The valuation note explains why Buffett could still hold a token stake, but it does not overturn the framework's PASS.

management_veto.summary: No management veto applied. This is a price-discipline rejection against a very high-quality staple franchise.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — respectable brand portfolio, but slower growth and still not cheap.
PASS — another excellent staple, but also priced below the framework's required return.

buffett_action: Berkshire reported 315,400 P&G shares worth about $43.9M at 2020-12-31 in its 13F-HR.
actual_result: That tiny stake remained unchanged at both 2021-12-31 and 2022-12-31. It looked like a legacy position, not a fresh high-conviction Berkshire move.
key_insight: World-class staple franchises can remain passes when the only thing clearly on offer is safety, not a sufficient forward return.
